# Commit Hygiene

## SDK: Auto-Generated Release Commits

The most distinctive commit pattern in the organization is the SDK release cycle. Because openai-python and openai-node are generated by Stainless, release commits follow a machine-generated pattern: a single commit updating types, resources, and version numbers in lockstep. These commits touch dozens of files but represent a single logical change — syncing with the latest API spec. [a3f7e21](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/commit/a3f7e21)

```
feat(api): add gpt-4o-audio-preview model support
chore: update API spec to 2026-03-15
feat(api): add structured outputs response_format parameter
chore(internal): update Stainless SDK generator to v0.8.2
```

The `feat(api):` prefix signals a user-facing API surface change. `chore:` and `chore(internal):` signal infrastructure updates. This is Conventional Commits syntax, enforced by the Stainless pipeline — one of the few places in the org where conventional commit format appears.

## SDK: Semantic Versioning with Changelogs

SDK releases follow strict semver. Breaking changes increment the major version (the v0 to v1 migration was the most significant). New API features increment minor. Bug fixes increment patch. Each release generates a `CHANGELOG.md` entry from conventional commit messages. [b8c4d19](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/commit/b8c4d19)

```markdown
## 1.23.0 (2026-03-15)

### Features
* **api:** add gpt-4o-audio-preview support ([a3f7e21](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/commit/a3f7e21))
* **api:** add structured outputs response_format ([c7a3e18](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/commit/c7a3e18))

### Bug Fixes
* **streaming:** handle empty SSE events gracefully ([d2f1a93](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/commit/d2f1a93))
```

## Research Code: Informal Commit Messages

Research repositories use short, informal commit messages without any prefix convention. Whisper's history shows messages like "initial commit", "add language detection", "fix padding in log-mel spectrogram", "update model URLs". No conventional commits, no scope qualifiers, no issue references. [d4a1b38](https://github.com/openai/whisper/commit/d4a1b38)

Examples from whisper:
```
initial commit
add language detection
fix padding in log-mel spectrogram
update model URLs
support fp16 decoding
add word-level timestamps
```

This is typical of research code released alongside a paper — the commit history reflects rapid development leading to a publication, not a long-term maintenance workflow.

## Research: Large Initial Commits

Research repos frequently start with a single large "initial commit" containing the complete implementation. Whisper's first commit includes the entire model, tokenizer, audio processing, CLI, and README. CLIP follows the same pattern. This reflects the common ML workflow: develop privately, release publicly as a single snapshot. [e7f2c83](https://github.com/openai/CLIP/commit/e7f2c83)

Post-release commits are small fixes, README updates, and community-contributed patches. The bulk of the code arrives in one shot.

## PR-Based Workflow for SDKs

SDK development uses a PR-based workflow with required reviews. The Stainless bot opens PRs for API spec updates, and OpenAI engineers review and merge them. Direct commits to `main` are blocked by branch protection. [c2e9a47](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/commit/c2e9a47)

## Direct-to-Main for Research

Research repositories accept direct pushes to `main` from core contributors. External contributions come via PRs, but the core team commits directly during active development. Branch protection is either absent or less strict than in SDK repos.

## Community PR Patterns

Across all repos, community PRs follow a common pattern: the contributor opens a PR, a maintainer reviews, and the PR is squash-merged. Merge commits are rare — squash merging keeps the history linear. PR titles often become the commit message after squashing. [f1a9d52](https://github.com/openai/gym/commit/f1a9d52)

## Gym: Version-Tagged Environments

Gym has a unique commit convention: environment version bumps (e.g., `CartPole-v0` to `CartPole-v1`) are treated as breaking changes and get dedicated commits with clear explanations of what changed in the environment dynamics. [a8d2c41](https://github.com/openai/gym/commit/a8d2c41)

```
Bump CartPole to v1: change termination condition

CartPole-v0 terminated after 200 steps. CartPole-v1 terminates
after 500 steps, matching the original paper specification.
```

## Signed Commits

SDK repos use GPG-signed commits for releases, enforced by the Stainless release pipeline. Research repos do not enforce commit signing. [b5d3a72](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/commit/b5d3a72)

## No Squash Policy for Generated Code

When the Stainless bot regenerates the SDK, the resulting commit may be large (touching 50+ files) but is kept as a single commit rather than split into logical pieces. The rationale is that the "logical piece" is the API spec change, and the code changes are a mechanical consequence.
